elements.sent-2411-001: We study the dynamics of synthetic molecules whose architectures are generated by space transformations from a point group acting on seed resonators.
elements.sent-2411-002: We show that the dynamical matrix of any such molecule can be reproduced as the left regular representation of a self-adjoint element from the stabilized group's algebra.
elements.sent-2411-003: Furthermore, we use elements of representation theory and K-theory to rationalize the dynamical features supported by such synthetic molecules up to topological equivalences.
elements.sent-2411-004: These tools enable us to identify a set of fundamental models which generate by superposition all possible dynamical matrices up to homotopy equivalences.
elements.sent-2411-005: Interpolations between these fundamental models give rise to topological spectral flows.
elements.sent-2411-006: The discovery of topological insulators \cite{Hal1988,KaneMele2005I,KaneMele2005II,BHZ2006,KWB2007, MooreBalents2007,FuKane2007,HQW2008 and of topological photonic and mechanical systems \cite{HaldaneRaghu2008,PP2009,WangNature2009,KaneNatPhys2013, HafeziNatPhot2013,NashPNAS2015,WuPRL2015,SusstrunkScience2015 , together with their complete classification \cite{SRFL2008,QiPRB2008,Kit2009,RSFL2010 , have permanently changed how research and discovery are conducted in materials science.}}}
elements.sent-2411-007: For example, the focus has shifted from enhancing the properties of a material to making it different.
elements.sent-2411-008: Furthermore, many applications rely now on robust effects that do not require fine tuning, such as generating wave channels at the interface between two topologically distinct materials, edge-to-edge topological pumping, or closing and opening of the resonant gaps by interpolating between two distinct material configurations.
elements.sent-2411-009: The latter application is called topological spectral engineering and we will see it at work in the present study.
elements.sent-2411-010: Besides supplying the means for important applications, observing a topological spectral flow is the simplest way to assess if two metamaterials are topologically distinct (see {\it e.g \cite{LuxJMP2024 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-011: These aforementioned robust effects are applicable to information and sensing technologies, where the main function of a device is to switch between on and off states as robustly as possible.
elements.sent-2411-012: Physical models in condensed matter physics are developed on periodic lattices $\Ll$ and most of the time they take the form of tight-binding Hamiltonians \begin{equation}\label{Eq:H} H = \sum_{x,y\in \Ll} t_{xy} \otimes |x\rangle \langle y| + t_{xy}^\ast \otimes |y\rangle \langle x|,\end{equation} where the intuition is that an electron hops between the orbitals supported by the sites of the lattice, with probabilities encoded in the matrices $t_{xy}$.
elements.sent-2411-013: In particular, the Hamiltonians of the established topological insulators are of this form (see \cite{RSFL2010 for complete list).}
elements.sent-2411-014: If one moves away from this context, unless specialized mathematical tools are utilized, it is not clear what a topological classification might be, how to write {\it all representative Hamiltonians, and how to compute topological invariants.}
elements.sent-2411-015: For example, statements like ``two spectrally gappped Hamiltonians are topologically equivalent if we can deform one into the other without closing the gap" are without value if the space in which the deformations occur is not specified.
elements.sent-2411-016: Imposing symmetry constraints on the Hamiltonians is still not enough because locality conditions on the hopping amplitudes are also needed.
elements.sent-2411-017: In a remarkable paper \cite{Bellissard1986 , Jean Bellissard realized that the full set of constraints can be communicated by simply stating that the Hamiltonians belong to a particular $C^\ast$-algebra.}
elements.sent-2411-018: The isomorphism class of this $C^\ast$-algebra identifies a specific class of materials and a concrete realization of it supplies the topological space for the deformations.
elements.sent-2411-019: These statements of Bellissard are not purely formal because, in the same paper, he showed how to construct such $C^\ast$-algebras (see also \cite{MeslandJGP2024 ).}
elements.sent-2411-020: Once the deformation space is identified, then various flavors of stable homotopy theories, aka K-theories, supply the devices that answer the classification problems.
elements.sent-2411-021: To the experts, the above statements and their implications are very clear, but this is not at all the case for a broader community of materials scientists, despite of many worked examples available in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-022: The authors believe that this current state of affairs is due to the fact that since these mentioned applications targeted challenging situations, there was no room for pedagogical expositions.
elements.sent-2411-023: As a result, the connections between the K-theoretic methods and other more familiar methods have not been revealed explicitly enough.
elements.sent-2411-024: The purpose of this expository paper is to fill in some of the mentioned shortcomings.
elements.sent-2411-025: The plan is to engage simple synthetic systems built from a finite number of self-coupling resonators and whose architecture is generated using the action of a finite subgroup of the full Euclidean group, hence, a point group (see section~\ref{Sec:SyntheticMolecules ).}
elements.sent-2411-026: The dynamical matrices determining the dynamical linear regimes of such systems all fall into the group algebra of the point group.
elements.sent-2411-027: As we shall see, we are in a situation where the representation theory of this group and the K-theory of its algebra overlap quite strongly (see section~\ref{Sec:AlgTopDyn ).}
elements.sent-2411-028: Thus, we are in a special situation where the K-theoretic tools can be seen at work through the prism of representation theory.
elements.sent-2411-029: For a community familiar with the latter, this class of physical systems and their analysis can be used as a door into the world of the K-theoretic ideas, which divert from representation theory as soon as the number of degrees of freedom become infinite.
elements.sent-2411-030: The exposition is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-031: Section~\ref{Sec:SyntheticMolecules describes quantum and classical materials generated from actions of the Euclidean group on seeding synthetic atoms (such as quantum dots) or self-coupling classical resonators (such as solid shapes fitted with magnets), respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-032: We use the terms synthetic molecules to refer to both these systems.
elements.sent-2411-033: Using simple physical reasoning, we calculate the algebra of the Hamiltonians.
elements.sent-2411-034: In the case where all the actions come from a subgroup of the Euclidean group, we find that this algebra drops to the group algebra of the space transformations.
elements.sent-2411-035: This, together with a natural topology on the algebra, completes the task of finding the deformation space for this particular class of synthetic molecules.
elements.sent-2411-036: Section~\ref{Sec:AlgTopDyn explains the interplay between representation theory and K-theory and demonstrates how numerical invariants are computed in general and in particular for setting of synthetic molecules.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Section~\ref{Sec:FundModels builds a set of Hamiltonians derived from K-theoretic data.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Any other Hamiltonian can be deformed to a direct sum of Hamiltonians from this list.
elements.sent-2411-039: Furthermore, we demonstrate that interpolations between pairs of Hamiltonians from the list result in topological spectral flows, hence confirming that they are topologically distinct.

elements.sent-2411-040: For simplicity, we describe these synthetic materials in a classical mechanical setting, but all our predictions apply in the acoustic, photonic and quantum settings as well.
elements.sent-2411-041: The synthetic mechanical materials that we have in mind are built from many copies of a single seed resonator.
elements.sent-2411-042: The seed resonator consists of moving parts, a physical frame and sources of force fields, such that:.
elements.sent-2411-043: The frame of the seed resonator can be decorated with sources of force fields, {\it e.g. magnets, in order to adjust its internal resonant modes and frequencies;.}
elements.sent-2411-044: The frame of the seed resonator can also be fitted with sensors in order to quantify the motion of its moving parts.
elements.sent-2411-045: The frames of the copies\footnote{A copy of the seed resonator will include the frame, the moving parts and all the other fittings.}
elements.sent-2411-046: The moving parts of the identical copies of the seed resonators self-couple via the force fields generated by the attached sources of force fields.
elements.sent-2411-047: A schematic example of such synthetic material is shown in Fig.~\ref{Fig:SymCp , and an actual laboratory model is shown and described in Fig.~\ref{Fig:RealSynthMolecule .}}
elements.sent-2411-048: We carefully listed our assumptions because they have several important consequences:.
elements.sent-2411-049: The physical frames and their fitted sensors supply local reference frames, marked by the red arrows in Fig.~\ref{Fig:SymCp , from where the motion is quantified.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Thus, if $\{q_\alpha\}$ are the generalized degrees of freedom of the seed resonator and $q_i(t)$ are their recorded values as function of time in an experiment, then same recordings will be reported if same experiment is carried out with the seed resonator placed at a different location and with different orientation.
elements.sent-2411-051: The collective motion of the self-coupled moving parts is fully determined by the positions and orientations of the frames.
elements.sent-2411-052: The motion, as quantified, or better said reported from the local frames, is unaffected by rigid translations and rotations of the whole structure, which is a consequence of Galilean invariance of the physical laws involved in the couplings.
elements.sent-2411-053: We will be dealing only with linear dynamical regimes, which requires the existence of stable equilibrium configurations.. Thus, we assume:.
elements.sent-2411-054: For each spatial configuration of the frames of the resonators, there is a unique lowest energy configuration.
elements.sent-2411-055: This assumption needs to be true only for the set of configurations sampled during an experiment or for the configurations engaged during an application.

elements.sent-2411-056: A somewhat trivial observation supplies the key to the operator algebraic approach we want to describe.
elements.sent-2411-057: Specifically, the position and orientation of the frame of a resonator can be set by a rotation followed by a translation of the seed resonator.
elements.sent-2411-058: These two space transformations define an element of the Euclidean group, which transforms the local reference frame of the seed resonator\footnote{All identical copies are generated from a seed resonator that is fixed once and for all at a specific location and orientation in space.}
elements.sent-2411-059: As such, there is a one-to-one correspondence between a resonator and an element of the Euclidean group.
elements.sent-2411-060: We reached our first important conclusion:.
elements.sent-2411-061: Then the architecture can be conceptualized as a discrete subset or better said a lattice $\Ll$ of the Euclidean group of space transformations.
elements.sent-2411-062: For example, these groups will supply space transformations on $\Ll$, but will also supply the labels (or the coordinates) for the points in $\Ll$.
elements.sent-2411-063: When appearing with an active role, the elements of the groups are indicated by $g$, and otherwise by $x$.. }$\Diamond$.
elements.sent-2411-064: We recall that the Euclidean group $\bm E$ is a topological group and that, for the Euclidean space with 3-dimensions, the underlying topological space is \begin{equation}\label{Eq:ETopo} \bm E \simeq \RM^3 \times \RM \mathbb P^3 \times \{-1,1\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-065: Note that $\RM \mathbb P^3 \times \{-1,1\}$ is the topological space underlying the subgroup $O(3)$ of rotations, with $\pm 1$ indicating if a rotation is proper or improper, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-066: According to our statement from above, the architecture of a synthetic molecule containing $N$ resonators can be quantified as a subset of $N$ distinct points on the space~\eqref{Eq:ETopo .}
elements.sent-2411-067: If a point of the 3-dimensional space is specified by a column vector with three entries encoding its coordinates, then the rotation transformations take the form of a matrix.
elements.sent-2411-068: Any proper rotation takes the form of a $3 \times 3$ matrix of the form \begin{equation}\label{Eq:Transf} R(\hat n, \theta) = \exp( \theta \, \hat n \cdot \vec L)\end{equation} where $\theta$ is an angle, $\hat n =(n_x,n_y,n_z)$ is 3-component vector of norm one and $\vec L$ is the 3-component vector with entries \begin{equation}L_x = { \begin{pmatrix} 0 0 0 0 0 -1 0 1 0 \end{pmatrix}}, \ L_y = { \begin{pmatrix} 0 0 1 0 0 0 -1 0 0 \end{pmatrix}}, \ L_z = { \begin{pmatrix} 0 -1 0 1 0 0 0 0 0 \end{pmatrix}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-069: Up to a factor, $\vec L$ coincides with the angular momentum in quantum mechanics for a particle with spin 1.
elements.sent-2411-070: The somewhat complicated space $\RM \mathbb P^3$ is a result of the allowed ranges for the pair $(\theta,\hat n)$ or, equivalently, for the vector $\vec n_\theta = \theta \hat n$.
elements.sent-2411-071: For $\|\vec n_\theta\| < \pi$, \eqref{Eq:Transf supplies distinct rotations, but for $\|\vec n_\theta\| = \pi$, \eqref{Eq:Transf supplies the same rotation if plug in $\vec n_\theta$ and $-\vec n_\theta$.}}
elements.sent-2411-072: Thus, rotations are fully parameterized by the points of a 3-dimensional ball of radius $\pi$ if the opposite points at its surface are identified.
elements.sent-2411-073: The resulting space is diffeomorphic to $\RM \mathbb P^3$, the space of lines in $\RM^4$ passing through the origin.. }$\Diamond$.

elements.sent-2411-074: So far, the discussion was about formalizing the architecture of given material.
elements.sent-2411-075: However, we can reverse the arrows and use Proposition~\ref{Prop:Architecture1 to generate interesting architectures.}

elements.sent-2411-078: We denote by $G$ the underlying group which labels the resonators of a synthetic molecule.
elements.sent-2411-079: Thus, $G$ can be the full Euclidean group or just its $O(3)$ subgroup.
elements.sent-2411-080: The seed resonator is labeled by the identity $e\in G$ and its degrees of freedom are $q(e,\alpha)$, $\alpha =1,\ldots,D$, with $D$ specifying the number of internal degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-081: Then the degrees of freedom of a resonator labeled by $x \in \Ll \subset G$ in the synthetic molecule are $q(x,\alpha)$, $\alpha=1,\ldots,D$.
elements.sent-2411-082: When the system is driven harmonically with pulsation $\omega$, under the assumption of very small but finite dissipation, the degrees of freedom display an oscillatory behavior as functions of time $t$, \begin{equation}q(x,\alpha;t) = Q_R(x,\alpha) \cos(\omega t + \phi_{x,\alpha}) = {\rm Re}[Q(x,\alpha) e^{\imath \omega t}],\end{equation} where $Q_R \in \RM$ are real-valued amplitudes, and $Q \in \CM$ are complex-valued amplitudes which efficiently incorporate the phases $\phi$.
elements.sent-2411-083: We can place the complex amplitudes in one single vector \begin{equation}\label{Eq:QVec} |Q\rangle = \sum_{x \in \Ll} \sum_{\alpha=1}^D Q(x,\alpha)|x,\alpha\rangle,\end{equation} where $|x,\alpha\rangle$ can be thought of as an abstract basis for the state space, or as a concrete column vector with entry 1 at one place and 0 entry at all other places.\footnote{The latter requires an (un-natural) ordering of $(x,\alpha)$'s and this is why the former viewpoint is preferred.}
elements.sent-2411-084: The vectors~\eqref{Eq:QVec span the Hilbert space $\ell^2(\Ll,\CM^d) \simeq \CM^D \otimes \ell^2(\Ll)$.}
elements.sent-2411-085: As a vector in $\CM^D \otimes \ell^2(\Ll)$, the dynamical state of the system takes the form $|Q\rangle = \sum_{x \in \Ll \bm Q_x \otimes |x \rangle$, with $\bm Q_x \in \CM^d$.}
elements.sent-2411-086: External generalized forces $\{f_{g,\alpha e^{\imath \omega t}\}$ driving the synthetic molecule can be also encoded as a vector from this Hilbert space, \begin{equation}|F\rangle = \sum_{x \in \Ll} f_{x,\alpha} |x,\alpha\rangle = \sum_{x\in \Ll} \bm f_x \otimes |x\rangle.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-087: If the quadratic form of the Lagrange function is \begin{equation}L(Q_R,\dot Q_R) = \tfrac{1}{2} \dot Q_R^T \bm T \dot Q_R + \tfrac{1}{2} Q_R^T \bm V Q_R,\end{equation} where $\bm T$ and $\bm V$ are positive operators on $\CM^D \otimes \ell^2(\Ll)$, then the response of the synthetic molecule to the driving forces is given by the solution to the equation \begin{equation}-\omega^2 \bm T |Q\rangle +\bm V |Q\rangle =|F\rangle \ \ \Leftrightarrow \ \ \bm T^{\frac{1}{2}}(\bm D - \omega^2)\bm T^{\frac{1}{2}} |Q \rangle = |F \rangle,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\bm D : = \bm T^{-\frac{1}{2}} \bm V \bm T^{-\frac{1}{2}}\end{equation} is call the dynamical matrix.
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}L(Q_R,\dot Q_R) = \tfrac{1}{2} \dot Q_R^T \bm T \dot Q_R + \tfrac{1}{2} Q_R^T \bm V Q_R,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-088: The resonant frequencies and the corresponding self-oscillating modes can be obtain from the spectral properties of $\bm D$, which, as any linear operator on the Hilbert space $\CM^D \otimes \ell^2(\Ll)$, it takes the form \begin{equation}\label{Eq:DMatrix} \bm D = \sum_{x,x'\in \Ll} w_{x',x}(\Ll) \otimes |x' \rangle \langle x|,\end{equation} where $w_{x',x}$ are $D \times D$ matrices with complex entries.
elements.sent-2411-089: Often, these are referred to as coupling matrices.
elements.sent-2411-090: Let us point out the similarity between the expressions \eqref{Eq:DMatrix and \eqref{Eq:H , where the only difference is that the lattices live in different topological groups, Euclidean group in the former and $\RM^3$ in the latter.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: The group $G$ can act on itself from the left or from the right and we will be interested in both.
elements.sent-2411-092: The left action is supplied by multiplication to the left, while the right action goes as $g\cdot x = x g^{-1}$, for all $g,x \in G$.
elements.sent-2411-093: These actions are also well defined on subsets of $G$, in particular, on $\Ll$.
elements.sent-2411-094: Now, in Eq.~\eqref{Eq:DMatrix , we were careful to specify that the coupling matrices of the synthetic molecule are entirely determined by the architecture of the molecule, hence by the lattice $\Ll$, as it was already explained in the previous subsection.}
elements.sent-2411-095: From the principles stated there, we can also infer that the coupling matrices satisfy the following covariant relation, \begin{equation}\label{Eq:CovRel} w_{g \cdot x',g \cdot x}(g \cdot \Ll) = w_{x',x}(\Ll),\end{equation} which is a direct consequence of the Galilean invariance of the physical laws underlining the coupling of the resonators.
elements.sent-2411-096: Taking $g = x$, one finds \begin{equation}w_{x',x}(\Ll) = w_{x \cdot x',e}(x \cdot \Ll),\end{equation} or equivalently, \begin{equation}\label{Eq:RedDMat} \bm D = \sum_{x,x'\in \Ll} w_{x \cdot x'}(x \cdot \Ll) \otimes |x'\rangle \langle x|,\end{equation} where we removed the redundant lower index.
elements.sent-2411-097: As shown in \cite{MeslandJGP2024 , this particular form of the dynamical matrices, together with the assumption that the coupling matrices depend continuously on the architecture, enable one to construct the $C^\ast$-algebra where the models live.}
elements.sent-2411-098: More precisely, if one keeps the architecture fixed but changes the internal structure of the seed resonator, the dynamical matrices densely sample a groupoid $C^\ast$-algebra that is entirely determined by the lattice $\Ll$.
elements.sent-2411-099: We discuss next how this $C^\ast$-algebra emerges in the particular setting.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Synthetic molecules generated with point groups
elements.sent-2411-100: The finite subgroups of $SO(3)$ are all known and classified \cite{SenechalAMM1990 .}
elements.sent-2411-101: In this subsection, we consider a seed resonator placed at a point in space and with an orientation described by $x_0 \in \bm E$.
elements.sent-2411-102: Copies of this seed resonator are then acted from the left with space transformations contained in a finite subgroup $\Gamma \subset SO(3)$.
elements.sent-2411-103: As such, we generate a finite lattice \begin{equation}\label{Eq:LGamma} \Ll =\{\gamma x_0, \ \gamma \in \Gamma\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-104: The seed resonator corresponds to the neutral element $e \in \Gamma$ and is part of lattice $\Ll$.
elements.sent-2411-105: This forbids $x_0$ for being part of the set $\bm E^\Gamma$ of points of $\bm E$ fixed by $\Gamma$.}$\Diamond$.
elements.sent-2411-106: We want to stress that resulting lattice depends quite sensitively on $x_0$, a fact that was already visible in Fig.~\ref{Fig:IHMolecule .}
elements.sent-2411-107: However, regardless of the initial choice of $x_0 \in \bm E \setminus \bm E^\Gamma$, we have:.
elements.sent-2411-108: The sets $x \cdot \Ll$ appearing in Eq.~\eqref{Eq:RedDMat are all identical.}
elements.sent-2411-109: Before giving the arguments, let us point out that $x \cdot \Ll$ for $x \in \Ll$ represents the lattice $\Ll$ as seen from the local reference frame of the resonator located at $x$.
elements.sent-2411-110: Then the statement says that synthetic molecule appears identical when looked at from any of the local reference frames.
elements.sent-2411-111: Now, if $x$ and $y$ belong to $\Ll$, they must be of the form $x=\gamma x_0$ and $y= \gamma' x_0$, for some $\gamma,\gamma' \in \Gamma$.. As such, \begin{equation}x \cdot y = y x^{-1}= (\gamma' x_0) (\gamma x_0)^{-1}= \gamma \cdot \gamma'.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-112: Given Eq.~\eqref{Eq:LGamma , we have \begin{equation}x \cdot \Ll = \gamma \cdot \Gamma = \Gamma,\end{equation} where we used the invariance of a group against its own right action.}
elements.sent-2411-113: The above facts have important consequences: Since the coupling matrices are entirely determined by the local environment as experienced from the local frames of the resonators, we have $w_{x',x (\Ll)=w_{x \cdot x'}$ and, by setting $x \cdot x' = \gamma$, we have \begin{equation}\label{Eq:DGamma} \bm D = \sum_{\gamma \in \Gamma} w_\gamma \otimes \sum_{x \in \Ll}|\gamma x\rangle \langle x|.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-114: We will see in the next section that any such $\bm D$ can be generated from the left-regular representations of the (stabilized) group $C^\ast$-algebra of $\Gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-115: As explained in our introductory remarks, this is an effective though somewhat abstract way to communicate the constraints we want to impose on our synthetic molecules.
elements.sent-2411-116: Under these constraints, one is free to modify the position $x_0$ of the seed resonator in $\bm E \setminus \bm E^\Gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-117: One is also free to change the internal structure of the seed resonator, including adding or removing degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-118: For example, we can add a second distinct seed resonator, because the pair can be seen as one seed resonator with a more complex structure.
elements.sent-2411-119: All these mentioned actions will essentially result in an alteration of the coupling matrices $w_\gamma$ and they lead to a well defined class of synthetic molecules associated with the group $\Gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-120: The common thing about these molecules is that their dynamical matrices are all generated from the same group $C^\ast$-algebra.
elements.sent-2411-121: Since there is no cap on the number of degrees of freedom carried by the seed resonator, one can create quite complicated molecules with the algorithm just described, with layers upon layers of resonators, each having their own spatial distributions.
elements.sent-2411-122: Due to this untamed complexity, one can be mislead into thinking that there are no limits on the eigenmodes or dynamical patterns one can produce with such molecules.
elements.sent-2411-123: Quite the contrary, once the point group $\Gamma$ is fixed, there are only a finite number of truly distinct dynamical patterns that one can squeeze out of the synthetic molecule (see section~\ref{Sec:FundModels ).}

elements.sent-2411-169: This section establishes the connection between the abstract $C^\ast$-algebra of a point group and the algebra of dynamical matrices for the class of synthetic molecules introduced in the previous section.
elements.sent-2411-170: The material of this subsection is standard and it can be found, for example, in \cite{DavidsonBook [Ch.~VIII].}
elements.sent-2411-171: Given a generic discrete group $\Gamma$, its group algebra $\CM \Gamma$ consists of formal series \begin{equation}\label{Eq:CG} q = \sum_{\gamma \in \Gamma} \alpha_\gamma \, \gamma, \quad \alpha_\gamma \in \CM,\end{equation} where all but a finite number of terms are zero.\footnote{Of course, this is of no concern if $\Gamma$ is finite.}
elements.sent-2411-172: The addition and multiplication of such formal series are defined in the obvious way, using the group and algebraic structures of $\Gamma$ and $\CM$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-173: In addition, there exists a natural $\ast$-operation \begin{equation}q^\ast = \sum_{\gamma \in \Gamma} \alpha_\gamma^\ast \, \gamma^{-1}, \quad (q^\ast)^\ast = q, \quad (\alpha q)^\ast = \alpha^\ast q^\ast, \ \alpha \in \CM.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-174: Hence, $\CM \Gamma$ is a $\ast$-algebra in a natural way.
elements.sent-2411-175: We denote by $e$ the neutral element of $\Gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-176: Then the map \begin{equation}\Tt : \CM \Gamma \to \CM, \quad \Tt(q) = \alpha_e\end{equation} defines a faithful positive trace on $\CM \Gamma$ and a pre-Hilbert structure on $\CM \Gamma$ via \begin{equation}\langle q, q' \rangle : = \Tt(q^\ast q'), \quad q,q' \in \CM \Gamma.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-177: The completion of the linear space $\CM G$ under this pre-Hilbert structure supplies the Hilbert space $\ell^2(\Gamma)$, spanned by $|\gamma\rangle$, $\gamma \in \Gamma$, which form an orthonormal basis \begin{equation}\langle \gamma , \gamma' \rangle = \delta_{\gamma,\gamma'}, \quad \gamma,\gamma' \in \Gamma.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-178: The left action of $\CM \Gamma$ on itself can be extended to the action of a bounded operator on $\ell^2(\Gamma)$, and this supplies the left regular representation $\pi_L$ of $\CM \Gamma$ inside the algebra $\BM\big(\ell^2(\Gamma)\big)$ of bounded operators on $\ell^2(\Gamma)$.
elements.sent-2411-180: The completion of $\CM \Gamma$ with respect to the norm \begin{equation}\label{Eq:CNorm} \|q\| : = \|\pi_L(q)\|_{\BM(\ell^2(\Gamma))}\end{equation} supplies the reduced group $C^\ast$-algebra $C^\ast_r \Gamma$ of $\Gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-181: However, the norm~\eqref{Eq:CNorm puts a topology on the algebra, which will then enable us to speak of ``continuous deformations'' of the models.. }$\Diamond$.
elements.sent-2411-182: The right-regular representation of the group algebra acts on $\ell^2(\Gamma)$ as \begin{equation}\label{Eq:PiRightReg} \pi_R(q) |\gamma'\rangle : = \sum_{\gamma \in \Gamma} \alpha_{\gamma} |\gamma' \gamma^{-1} \rangle, \quad q \in C^\ast_r \Gamma, \quad \gamma' \in \Gamma.\end{equation}.
elements.subsection-2411-006: Symmetry and structure of dynamical matrices
elements.sent-2411-183: The right-regular representation induces a unitary representation of $\Gamma$ on $\ell^2(\Gamma)$: \begin{equation}U : \Gamma \to \BM\big ( \ell^2(\Gamma) \big ), \quad U_\gamma |\gamma' \rangle : = \pi_R(\gamma)|\gamma'\rangle = |\gamma' \gamma^{-1}\rangle.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-184: Since the left and right actions commute, we automatically have that \begin{equation}U_\gamma^\ast \, \pi_L(q) \, U_\gamma = \pi_L(q), \quad q \in C^\ast_r \Gamma, \quad \gamma \in \Gamma.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-185: Hence, the group $C^\ast$-algebra supplies models that are naturally $\Gamma$-symmetric.

elements.section-2411-004: Algebraic and Topological Aspects of the Dynamics
elements.sent-2411-192: The subject of this section is on the decomposition of the linear space of resonant modes in the smallest possible subspaces that remain invariant under dynamics, as well as on the classification of these subspaces from algebraic and topological point of views.
elements.sent-2411-193: The former one engages the ordinary representation theory while the latter one requires more specialized tools, specifically, Kasparov's bi-variant K-theory.
elements.sent-2411-194: While an in-depth mastery of the latter requires substantial effort, the concepts and the working principles can be communicated in very few words.
elements.sent-2411-195: For a reader unfamiliar with the theory, seeing them in action in the simplest but non-trivial context of finite groups and algebras can serve as a door to the world of these ideas.
elements.sent-2411-196: This is one of the main reasons KK-theory appears in our exposition.
elements.sent-2411-197: Another reason is that there is no other natural alternative to the topological point of view.
elements.sent-2411-198: In our opinion, KK-theory is a must-have tool for anyone planning to enter the business of topological dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-199: We hope that the patient reader can sense that from the exposition below.
elements.sent-2411-200: A unitary representation of a group $\Gamma$ is a group morphism between the group and the group $\UM(\Hh)$ of unitary operators over a Hilbert space $\Hh$.
elements.sent-2411-201: Thus, a unitary representation $\pi : \Gamma \to \UM(\Hh)$, typically shorthanded to just $\pi$, specifies both the Hilbert space $\Hh$ and the morphism itself.
elements.sent-2411-202: If the Hilbert space has a finite dimension $N$, then we are talking about finite dimensional representations, which are nothing but group morphisms from $\Gamma$ to $U(N)$, the group of unitary matrices of rank $N$.
elements.sent-2411-203: Two representations $\pi_1: \Gamma \to \UM(\Hh_1)$ and $\pi_2: G \to \UM(\Hh_2)$ are said to be unitarily equivalent if there exists a unitary map $U: \Hh_1 \to \Hh_2$ such that $\pi_1 = U^\ast \pi_2 U$.
elements.sent-2411-204: Representation theory is the art of classifying the representations of a group up to unitary equivalence.
elements.sent-2411-205: Since the relation between finite groups and their group algebras is functorial, any finite group representation of a finite group extends by linearity to a finite representation of its algebra as bounded linear operators over the same Hilbert space.
elements.sent-2411-206: Reciprocally, any finite representation of a group's algebra supplies, by restriction to the group elements, a representation of the group.
elements.sent-2411-207: Given two finite group representations $\pi_1$ and $\pi_2$, we can define a new finite representation by using their direct sum $\pi_1 \oplus \pi_2$.
elements.sent-2411-208: This binary operation is compatible with the unitary equivalence, hence it descends on the set of unitarily equivalent classes of finite representations, where it becomes an abelian binary operation.
elements.sent-2411-209: Thus, the set $\Rr$ of classes of unitarily equivalent finite group representations has a natural structure of an abelian semigroup.
elements.sent-2411-210: It can be closed to a group $(\Rr,\oplus)$, which is called the group of virtual representations.

elements.sent-2411-211: A representation $\pi: \Gamma \to \UM(\Hh)$ is said to be irreducible if there is no linear subspace of $\Hh$ that is left invariant by the actions of all $\pi(\gamma)$, $\gamma\in \Gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-212: Up to unitary equivalences, the irreducible representations of a finite group $\Gamma$ can be enumerated using the characters of the group, which are readily available in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-213: For example, the table of characters of the proper icosahedral group can be found in \cite{LiboffBook and is reproduced in Table~\ref{Tb:1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-214: There is a simple and practical way to generate an irreducible representation $\pi_\chi$ from a character $\chi$ (see section~\ref{Sec:GenFM ).}
elements.sent-2411-215: Any finite group representation $\pi$ is unitarily equivalent to a finite direct sum of irreducible representations \begin{equation}\pi \simeq \bigoplus\nolimits_\chi (\pi_\chi \oplus \cdots \oplus \pi_\chi)\end{equation} The above can be written more compactly as $\pi \simeq \oplus_\chi \, n_\chi \, \pi_\chi$, $n_\chi \in \NM$, which shows that $(\Rr,\oplus)$ is freely and commutatively generated over the classes of irreducible representations.
elements.sent-2411-216: There is additional algebraic structure on $\Rr$.
elements.sent-2411-217: Indeed, given two finite group representations $\pi_1$ and $\pi_2$, we can generate a new finite representation using their tensor product $\pi_1 \otimes \pi_2$.
elements.sent-2411-218: This binary operation is also compatible with the unitary equivalence, hence it descends on $\Rr$, where it becomes an associative commutative binary operation, which distributes over $\oplus$.
elements.sent-2411-219: Thus, the set $\Rr$ of classes of unitarily equivalent finite group virtual representations has a natural structure of a commutative ring, $(\Rr,\oplus,\otimes)$, called the ring of virtual representations \cite{Serre1977LinearRO .}
elements.sent-2411-220: When analyzing the dynamics of a synthetic molecule, the ring of representations comes into play in the following way.
elements.sent-2411-221: Let $\bm D$ be a dynamical matrix as in \eqref{Eq:DGamma .}
elements.sent-2411-222: As we have already seen, $\bm D$ is a linear operator over the Hilbert space $\Hh=\CM^D \otimes \ell^2(\Gamma)$, which is necessarily the left-regular representation of an element $h$ from the group algebra $\CM \Gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-223: Suppose we compute or measure the resonant spectrum of $\bm D$ and we find an eigenvalue $\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-224: By standard methods, we can associate a spectral projection $P_\lambda$ such that $P_\lambda \Hh$ is the linear space spanned by all the eigenvectors of $\bm D$ corresponding to $\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-225: One finds that this $P_\lambda$ is also the left-regular representation of a projection $p_\lambda$ from $\CM \Gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-226: For example, consider the so-called interpolating polynomials defined by the relations $F_\lambda(\lambda') = \delta_{\lambda,\lambda'}$ for any $\lambda$ and $\lambda'$ from the discrete spectrum of $\bm D = \pi_L(h)$.
elements.sent-2411-227: Recalling that the finite representations commute with polynomial calculus, we have \begin{equation}P_{\lambda} = F_{\lambda}(\bm D) = F_{\lambda}(\pi_L(h))=\pi_L(F_{\lambda}(h)),\end{equation} which shows that $P_{\lambda}$ is the left-regular representation of $p_{\lambda =F_{\lambda (h) \in \CM \Gamma$.}}
elements.sent-2411-228: Lastly, since the left and right regular representations commute, we see that \begin{equation}\label{Eq:PiLambda} \pi_\lambda: \Gamma \to \BM(P_\lambda \Hh), \quad \pi_\lambda (\gamma) : = P_\lambda \pi_R(\gamma) P_\lambda,\end{equation} defines a finite representation of $\Gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-229: We learned in the previous sub-section that each $\pi_\lambda$ is unitarily equivalent to a direct sum of irreducible representations.
elements.sent-2411-230: Thus, we have the following inescapable fact:.
elements.sent-2411-231: Up to a unitary transformation, all resonant modes, without exception, coincide with a vector from the representation space of one of the $\pi_\chi$'s, or linear combinations of such vectors.
elements.sent-2411-232: The above fact shows that the dynamical patterns supported by a synthetic molecule from the class associated to the group $\Gamma$ can be all generated as linear combinations of vectors drawn from a finite number of spaces, which can be enumerated by the character of the group.
elements.sent-2411-233: The statement is purely algebraic and, as such, it cannot reveal anything about the stability of those dynamical patterns against continuous deformations of the molecule.
elements.sent-2411-234: In the light of this remark, it is then natural to approach the representation theory and the classification of dynamical patterns from a topological point of view.
elements.sent-2411-235: This is the subject of the following two subsections.
elements.sent-2411-236: We describe here the so called ring of Fredholm representations introduced by Kasparov \cite{KasparovConspectus .}
elements.sent-2411-237: This is achieved through Kasparov's bi-variant K-theory \cite{KasparovJSM1981,KasparovJSM1987 , also known as KK-theory, which has many equivalent presentations.}
elements.sent-2411-238: In Cuntz's picture \cite{CuntzKTheory1987 , for two $C^\ast$-algebras $A$ and $B$, an element of Kasparov's abelian group $KK_0(A,B)$ is presented as the homotopy class of a pair of morphisms $\bar \phi =(\phi_1,\phi_2)$ from $A$ to the algebra of adjointable endomorphisms over the standard Hilbert $C^\ast$-module $\ell^2(\NM,B)$, with the property that the difference $\phi_1 -\phi_2$ takes values in $\KM \otimes B$.}
elements.sent-2411-239: In particular, any morphism $\psi : A \to \KM \otimes B$ supplies the element $[(\psi,0)]$ of $KK_0(A,B)$, where $[\cdot]$ indicates the homotopy class.
elements.sent-2411-240: The abelian group structure on $KK_0(A,B)$, denoted by ``+'' in the following, is induced by the direct sum of algebra morphisms.
elements.sent-2411-241: A powerful feature of Kasparov's K-theory is the existence of multiplicative structures.
elements.sent-2411-242: If $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$ are $C^\ast$-algebras, then there is an associative internal product \begin{equation}KK_0(A,B) \times KK_0(B,C) \to KK_0(A,C)\end{equation} and an associative external product \begin{equation}KK_0(A,B) \otimes KK_0(C,D) \to KK_0(A\otimes C, B \otimes D).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-243: In particular, if $\psi : A \to B$ and $\psi' : B \to C$ are $C^\ast$-algebra morphisms, then \begin{equation}[\psi ] \times [\psi'] = [\psi' \circ \psi],\end{equation} and if $\psi : A \to C$ and $\psi' : B \to D$ are $C^\ast$-algebra morphisms, then \begin{equation}[\psi ] \otimes [\psi'] = [\psi \otimes \psi'].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-244: See \cite{KasparovJSM1981,KasparovJSM1987 or the textbook \cite{BlackadarBook for more details.}}
elements.sent-2411-245: However, for the present context, the special cases mentioned above suffice (see subsection~\ref{Sec:Inv ).}
elements.sent-2411-246: A curious reader can find in \cite{ProdanRMP2016 explicit algorithms for other situations that often occur in physical applications.. }$\Diamond$.
elements.sent-2411-247: To introduce the ring of Fredholm representations of a group $\Gamma$, we focus on $KK_0(C^\ast_r \Gamma, \CM)$.
elements.sent-2411-248: Note that the elements of $KK_0(C^\ast_r \Gamma, \CM)$ are pairs $(\phi_1,\phi_2)$ of algebra morphisms from $C^\ast_r \Gamma$ to $\BM(\Hh)$, the algebra of bounded operators over a separable Hilbert space, such that $\phi_1 -\phi_2$ lands in $\KM(\Hh)$, the subalgebra of compact operators.
elements.sent-2411-249: This difference is erased if we take the quotient of $\BM(\Hh)$ by $\KM(\Hh)$ and, as such, the two morphisms $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$ are identical as morphisms into the Calkin algebra $\QM(\Hh) \simeq \BM(\Hh)/\KM(\Hh)$.
elements.sent-2411-250: Restricting to the group elements, this provides a representation of the group $\Gamma$ inside the group of invertible elements of $\QM(\Hh)$, that is, in the group of Fredholm operators.
elements.sent-2411-251: Hence, the name of Fredholm representations.
elements.sent-2411-252: The ring of Fredholm representations exists for general locally compact groups but its construction in this general setting requires equivariant KK-theory.
elements.sent-2411-253: The latter is more technical and harder to explain with few words than the ordinary KK-theory outlined above.
elements.sent-2411-254: For this reason, we follow a construction due to Cuntz \cite{CuntzJRAM1983 , which works for discrete groups, finite or not.}
elements.sent-2411-255: In this approach, one uses both the internal and external products to define the ring structure.
elements.sent-2411-256: The key is to see that the group morphism $\Gamma \ni \gamma \mapsto (\gamma,\gamma) \in \Gamma \times \Gamma$ can be lifted to a morphism of $C^\ast$-algebras\footnote{This morphism supplies the standard co-product on $C^\ast_r \Gamma$.. \begin{equation}C^\ast_r \Gamma \ni q=\sum_\gamma \alpha_\gamma \, \gamma \mapsto \eta(q) = \sum_\gamma \alpha_\gamma \, \gamma \otimes \gamma \in C^\ast_r \Gamma \otimes C^\ast_r \Gamma \simeq C^\ast_r(\Gamma \times \Gamma).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-257: Furthermore, the homotopy class $[\eta]$ supplies an element of $KK_0(C^\ast_r \Gamma, C^\ast_r \Gamma \otimes C^\ast_r \Gamma)$.
elements.sent-2411-258: Then, given two Fredholm representations, hence two members $[\phi]$ and $[\phi']$ of $KK_0(C^\ast_r \Gamma, \CM)$, we can first take their external product $[\phi] \otimes [\phi']$, to land in $KK_0(C^\ast_r \Gamma \otimes C^\ast_r \Gamma, \CM)$, and then take the internal product of the result with $[\eta]$ on the left, to land back in $KK_0(C^\ast_r \Gamma, \CM)$.
elements.sent-2411-259: In this way, one defines the product \begin{equation}KK_0(C^\ast_r \Gamma, \CM) \ni [\phi], [\phi'] \mapsto [\phi] \cdot [\phi'] : = [\eta] \times ([\phi] \otimes [\phi']) \in KK_0(C^\ast_r \Gamma, \CM),\end{equation} which is a commutative binary operation that distributes over abelian group structure of $KK_0(C^\ast_r \Gamma, \CM)$ \cite{CuntzJRAM1983 .}
elements.sent-2411-260: The resulting ring structure $(KK_0(C^\ast_r \Gamma, \CM),+,\cdot)$ is the ring of Fredholm representations.
elements.sent-2411-261: It supplies the means to investigate the representation theory of a group, this time using homotopy instead of unitary equivalence.
elements.sent-2411-262: We have the following important fact:.
elements.sent-2411-263: As such, $KK_0(C^\ast_r \Gamma, \CM)$ is generated by the homotopy classes $[\pi_\chi:C^\ast_r \Gamma \to \KM]$ of the irreducible representations of the group, as lifted to $C^\ast \Gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-264: The main point here is that we replaced the unitary equivalence with the homotopy equivalence of representations.
elements.sent-2411-265: This, for example, gives us automatic assurance that the representation~\eqref{Eq:PiLambda stays in the same class as long as the projection $p_\lambda$ varies continuously.}
elements.sent-2411-266: This is the case if the dynamical matrix is deformed continuously and the eigenvalue $\lambda$ stays isolated.
elements.sent-2411-267: Furthermore, the homotopy classes of the representations act as topological charges for the energy levels.
elements.sent-2411-268: Indeed, when two or more energy levels collide and split again during a deformation, we can be sure that the net topological charge carried by the levels is conserved.
elements.sent-2411-269: Thus, the topological charges are never lost but are redistributed during collisions of energy levels.
elements.sent-2411-270: K-theory is the art of classifying projections with respect to stable homotopy.
elements.sent-2411-271: Textbooks offering introductions to the subject are the monograph by Efton Park \cite{ParkBook , for topological spaces, and the one by Rordam and Larsen \cite{RordamBook , for $C^\ast$-algebras.}}

elements.sent-2411-273: A projection from a generic $C^\ast$-algebra is an element $p$ with the properties $p^2=p^\ast=p$.
elements.sent-2411-274: Given the group $\Gamma$, we have seen that the $C^\ast$-algebra covering all dynamical matrices of molecules from the class associated to $\Gamma$ is $\KM \otimes C^\ast_r \Gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-275: We will organize the projections from this algebra by the homotopy equivalence which says that $p \sim_h p'$ iff there exists a continuous family $p(t)$ of projections in $\KM \otimes C^\ast_r \Gamma$ such that $p(0)=p$ and $p(1)=p'$.
elements.sent-2411-276: Note that along such homotopy, the number of engaged degrees of freedom can vary.
elements.sent-2411-277: Thus, the stated equivalence relation is stronger than the homotopy equivalence defined for a fixed number of degrees of freedom, {\it i.e. with deformations occurring in $M_{D \times D (\CM) \otimes C^\ast_r \Gamma$ with a fixed $D$.}}

elements.sent-2411-304: Consider a symmetric Hamiltonian $H$ on $\CM^N \otimes \ell^2(\Gamma)$ with a gap in its spectrum and denote by $P_G$ the spectral projection onto the spectrum below this gap.

elements.sent-2411-308: Thus, if we define the Hamiltonians $H_\chi = - \pi_L(p_\chi)$, then, up to continuous deformation, $H$ is equivalent to a direct sum of $H_\chi$'s.
elements.sent-2411-309: In other words, any class of gapped Hamiltonians can represented by a stack of disconnected models $H_\chi$.
elements.sent-2411-310: Hence, the latter can be rightfully called the fundamental models in the context of $\Gamma$-symmetric models.
elements.sent-2411-311: The goal of this section is to supply techniques that generate explicit expressions for $H_\chi$'s that involve a small number of entries.
elements.sent-2411-312: Our first task is to supply an explicit realization of a generating set $\{p_\chi\}$ of projections for the $K_0$-group.
elements.sent-2411-313: Consider a generic self-adjoint element from $\CM \Gamma$, $h = \sum_{g \in \Gamma \alpha_\gamma \cdot \gamma$.}
elements.sent-2411-314: Since the sum involves $|\Gamma|$ complex coefficients, we will view $h$ as living in the $\CM^{|\Gamma|}$ parameter space.
elements.sent-2411-315: The left-regular representation $\pi_L(h)$ on the Cayley graph of $\Gamma$ has a pure-point spectrum and the eigen-spaces corresponding to the eigenvalues supply irreducible representations for $\Gamma$, unless the coefficients $\alpha_\gamma$ enter in specific relations.
elements.sent-2411-316: In other words, the eigen-spaces $h$ supply irreducible representations for $\Gamma$ except when the set of coefficients $\{\alpha_\gamma\}$ belongs to a sub-manifold of the full parameter space of strictly smaller dimension.
elements.sent-2411-317: As such, if we generate the coefficients $\alpha_\gamma$ from a ball of $\CM^{|\Gamma|}$ via a random process, then, with probability one, the eigen-spaces of $h$ will supply irreducible representations of $\Gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-318: Since each of them shows up in the regular representation of the group, this technique gives us a practical way to sample all irreducible representations of $\Gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-319: If $\lambda_\chi$ and its spectral projector \begin{equation}P_\chi = \sum_{\pi_L(h)\ket{\eta^{(\chi)}_i} = \lambda_\chi \ket{\eta^{(\chi)}_i}} \ket{\eta^{(\chi)}_i}\bra{\eta^{(\chi)}_i}\end{equation} were identified to relate to a representation $\chi$, then the sought projection can be computed as \begin{equation}\label{Eq:PChi2} p_\chi = \sum_{\gamma \in \Gamma} \langle e | P_\chi | \gamma \rangle \, \gamma.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-320: For the proper icosahedral group $I_p$, we don't need to look any further than the adjacency operator $\Delta$ because each irreducible representation of $I_p$ appears as one of its eigen-spaces.
elements.sent-2411-321: While our goal for the section is theoretically completed, the solution we have now is not entirely satisfactory from the practical point of view.
elements.sent-2411-322: Indeed, if we want to implement $H_\chi = - \pi_R(p_\chi)$ experimentally, we will find it quite difficult because each term appearing in the sum~\eqref{Eq:PChi2 requires a physical coupling.}
elements.sent-2411-323: Thus, to reach a practical level, we must reduce the number of terms in Eq.~\eqref{Eq:PChi2 as much as possible, without changing the topological class of $H_\chi$.}
elements.sent-2411-324: For this purpose, we present two methods.
elements.sent-2411-325: The first method rests on the simple observation that the self-adjoint operator $(h-\lambda_\chi)^2$ has a positive spectrum which contains $0$ and the spectral projector corresponding to the $0$ eigenvalue coincides with $P_\chi$.
elements.sent-2411-326: Then we can choose $h_\chi = (h-\lambda_\chi)^2$ if we manage to generate a $h \in \CM G$ with a short coupling range.
elements.sent-2411-327: With the proposed random process introduced above, this can be achieved by sampling the elements with coupling range 1, moving to elements with coupling range 2 if not successful, and so on.
elements.sent-2411-328: In the case of the proper icosahedral group, the adjacency operator~\eqref{Eq:Adjacency has coupling range 1, hence it is optimal and we can choose $h_\chi = (\Delta - \lambda_\chi)^2$, which has coupling range 2.}

elements.sent-2411-343: In this subsection, we demonstrate that interpolations between pairs of distinct fundamental models generate topological spectral flows that are robust against perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-344: To start, we perturb $h_\chi$ from equation \eqref{eqn:toymodel as \begin{equation}\label{eqn:compactpertubation} h_\chi + s\sum_{\gamma \in \Gamma} \tfrac{1}{2}(\alpha_\gamma + \bar \alpha_{\gamma^{-1}}) \gamma = h_\chi + s K_\chi,\end{equation} where the complex coefficients $\alpha_\gamma$ were drawn randomly from the unit disk and $s$ is a parameter that controls the strength of the perturbation.}
elements.sent-2411-345: From here on, all of the perturbed $h_\chi$'s will be rescaled so that their lowest spectral gap is unity.
elements.sent-2411-346: Since there are five topologically distinct fundamental models, we can generate ten distinct homotopies between pairs, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{Fig:LabellingScheme .}
elements.sent-2411-347: The spectral flows generated by these interpolations are reported in Fig.~\ref{Fig:Interpolation1 .}
elements.sent-2411-348: All spectral flows confirm the expected closings of lowest spectral gaps.
